One fairness argument says: the five and the one have equal moral status; none is liable; so you shouldn’t impose death on one to save five, because that treats the one as having less claim. The fact that there are more on one side doesn’t automatically make it fair to kill the one. But then a competing fairness argument says: it’s fair to minimize harm because each person has an equal claim not to be killed, and satisfying more claims is better. You’re not treating the one as less valuable; you’re responding to the structure of the situation where you cannot satisfy all claims. Some propose a lottery: if you must kill someone, randomize. But in the standard trolley, you don’t have time or mechanism for a lottery; the lever is the only control. Also, if you did nothing, you’re effectively “choosing” the five by default. Defaulting to inaction is not obviously fairer; it just privileges the status quo trajectory. So fairness doesn’t settle it. It highlights that “numbers count” is not obviously compatible with equal respect unless you have an argument for why saving more is a form of respect for each person’s claim. Contractualists sometimes give that argument by appealing to ex ante reasons: before you know whether you’ll be in the group of five or the group of one, you’d prefer a principle that saves the greater number in symmetric cases.
Ex ante reasoning sounds like expected utility. But contractualists say they don’t aggregate. Isn’t that inconsistent?

It’s a tension in the literature. Some contractualists try to avoid full aggregation but still allow “pairwise comparisons” or “generic reasons.” One move: Instead of summing welfare, consider whether a principle is justifiable to each person given that they could occupy any position. If the situation is symmetric and no one is singled out, a principle that minimizes expected risk of death might be justifiable. But critics say that’s just aggregation in disguise: you’re effectively using probabilistic aggregation across possible selves. Scanlon sometimes allows aggregation in cases where harms are of the same kind and imposed in a way that doesn’t involve special wrongs like using someone as a means. Trolley cases become the battleground for how much aggregation is permissible. So yes, ex ante reasoning borrows from decision theory. Contractualists differ on whether that’s legitimate.
Speaking of decision theory, could we model trolley choices as problems of rational choice under moral constraints? Like a constrained optimization?

Yes. You can treat morality as defining a feasible set of actions, and within that set you optimize some value (like lives saved). That’s a common way to formalize deontological constraints. For example: - Objective: minimize deaths. - Constraint: do not intentionally kill an innocent person as a means. - Constraint: do not violate certain rights. In standard switch: - Action “pull” satisfies constraint (if you interpret it as not using as means) and yields 1 death. - Action “do nothing” satisfies constraints and yields 5 deaths. So “pull” is chosen. In fat man: - Action “push” violates constraint (intentional killing as means). - Action “do nothing” doesn’t violate it. So “do nothing,” even though deaths are higher. This formalization makes explicit why means/side-effect matters: it changes which actions are feasible. You can also introduce thresholds: if deaths prevented exceed some number, constraints can be overridden. That yields a smooth model of why people might accept pushing if it saves thousands. But the hard part is specifying the constraints non-arbitrarily.
I want to avoid personal dilemmas, but can we explore the “threshold” idea abstractly? At what point would it be permissible to push? If ever?

Abstractly, threshold deontology says: constraints against certain actions (like killing as a means) are very strong but not absolute; beyond some catastrophic threshold, consequences override. So you might have: - For small numbers (5 vs 1): constraint holds, don’t push. - For huge numbers (e.g., many thousands vs 1): perhaps you may push. The challenge is that the threshold seems morally arbitrary: why does a constraint suddenly collapse at N? Some try to avoid a sharp cutoff by having “weighted” reasons that can be outweighed gradually, but then it starts looking like consequentialism with extra terms. Still, threshold deontologists argue that absolute constraints lead to fanaticism: refusing to violate a constraint even to prevent catastrophic harm seems irrational or morally perverse. Others embrace absolutism: it is never permissible to intentionally kill an innocent person as a means, regardless of numbers. They accept that morality can demand saintly restraint. So whether “ever” depends on your foundational commitments. If you want a theoretically neat view, absolutism is simpler. If you want a view that aligns with many people’s intuitions about catastrophe, threshold views fit better but are messier.
Another thing: people mention “omission bias” and “status quo bias” as psychological explanations. Do these undermine the moral distinctions?

They can undermine confidence that the distinctions are morally fundamental. Omission bias: people judge harms caused by action as worse than equivalent harms caused by omission. Status quo bias: people prefer not to change the current trajectory. In trolley, doing nothing preserves the status quo trajectory (trolley continues) and is an omission; pulling is an intervention. These biases could make people more reluctant to act in some variants, or to push. But in the standard switch, people often do act, which suggests those biases aren’t dominating there. They might dominate in “footbridge” pushing, where action is more salient and violent. Psychological explanations don’t automatically debunk moral distinctions; they show that intuitions might be influenced by non-moral factors. The philosophical task is then to decide which intuitions to trust and which to revise. Some philosophers adopt “reflective equilibrium”: adjust principles and judgments until they cohere. If you think omission bias is irrational, you might discount intuitions driven by it.
Reflective equilibrium seems like a fancy way of saying “massage your intuitions until they fit your theory.” Is there a more rigorous method?

There are more rigorous approaches, but none is purely algorithmic. 1) Conceptual analysis with formal tools: use causal models, counterfactual tests, and clear definitions to see whether distinctions are coherent. 2) Debunking arguments: show that a judgment is produced by an unreliable process (e.g., evolved violence aversion) and therefore has reduced evidential weight. But this is controversial because many moral judgments have evolutionary origins. 3) Wide reflective equilibrium: include not just intuitions about cases, but theoretical virtues (simplicity, explanatory power), empirical facts about human psychology, and social considerations. 4) Constructivist methods: derive principles from conditions of agency or public reason, rather than from intuitions. Kantian constructivism and contractualism aim for this. But even these approaches often consult intuitions at some stage, because we need some data about what counts as a moral reason. So the critique has bite: trolley cases expose how much moral philosophy relies on considered judgments.

Let’s examine the “moral luck” angle. In the lever case, whether the side track has one person is luck. If it were empty, pulling would be obviously right. So is the wrongness (if any) dependent on luck?

Good point. Moral luck concerns whether moral assessment depends on factors beyond the agent’s control. In switch: - If the side track is empty, pulling saves five with no death. - If it has one person, pulling kills one. If your decision procedure is “pull to divert away from five,” then the moral outcome depends on luck about who is on the side track. Some argue that moral assessment should focus on the agent’s choice under uncertainty: if you reasonably believe the side track is empty, pulling is fine. If you know it’s occupied, you face a different choice. So the moral luck issue arises mostly when outcomes differ despite identical intentions and reasonable beliefs. In trolley cases, we usually stipulate full knowledge, so it’s less about luck and more about deliberate trade-offs. But you can create a luck variant: you can pull a lever that randomly sends the trolley to one of two side tracks, one empty, one with a person, but you don’t know which. Then your action risks killing. Is it permissible? That becomes a risk ethics problem.
Risk ethics seems relevant. Maybe pushing is worse because it’s a sure intentional killing, while pulling could be seen as choosing the lesser risk? But in the standard case it’s certain too.

Right: standard switch has certainty—pulling certainly kills one. So risk doesn’t distinguish it. But risk ethics can illuminate policy versions: if you adopt a rule that sometimes authorizes pushing, you increase risk of abuse and misapplication. That could be why rule utilitarians and contractualists resist it. At the one-off level, the difference is more about structure than risk.
I want to ask about the “principle of permissible harm” in war, like collateral damage. Is switch like collateral damage and fat man like terrorism?

That analogy is often drawn. - Collateral damage: civilians die as a foreseen side effect of attacking a military target. - Terrorism: civilians are intentionally targeted as a means to achieving political aims (e.g., to instill fear). The moral distinction many accept mirrors DDE: side-effect harm can be permissible under proportionality; intentional targeting of innocents is impermissible. Switch resembles collateral damage: the one is not targeted as a tool; their death is a side effect of redirecting the threat. Fat man resembles terrorism: the person is used as a means. Of course, the analogy is imperfect: trolley cases are immediate and local; war involves complex agency and uncertainty. But the structural similarity is part of why trolley thought experiments are taken seriously—they model core distinctions used in applied ethics.
That helps. But then critics of DDE in war say the distinction is bogus because you can always redescribe intentions. If I bomb a city to break morale, is that intended or foreseen? It blurs.

Exactly. Intention can be manipulable by description, and real-world cases are messy. Defenders of DDE respond by emphasizing: - The agent’s deliberative plan and what they choose as the route. - The proportionality and necessity conditions: even side-effect harms must be minimized and not excessive relative to the good achieved. But critics argue that “foreseen but not intended” can become a moral loophole. If you reliably foresee civilian deaths and proceed, saying you “don’t intend” them may be self-serving. This is why some try to ground the distinction in causal structure rather than intention, or in public justifiability: can you publicly defend your action without relying on dubious mental-state claims? Trolley cases abstract away from these complications, but your point stands: the more you rely on intention, the more you face redescription problems.
So what’s the best current view? Is there any consensus in philosophy?

There’s no consensus. But there are stable “packages” of views: 1) Consequentialist package: lever and push are morally equivalent; do what minimizes deaths. Intuitions against pushing are biases. 2) Deontological package (DDE/means): lever permissible, push impermissible, loop impermissible. The key is not using a person as a means. 3) Contractualist/public reason package: lever may be justifiable; push not, because principles permitting instrumental killing are reasonably rejectable. 4) Skeptical package: trolley intuitions are unreliable; the cases are too artificial to support deep conclusions. Still, they can clarify concepts. Within academia, many find means/side-effect and instrumentalization compelling but acknowledge the theory is incomplete and challenged. If you want a “best” view, it depends on what you prioritize: theoretical simplicity (consequentialism), alignment with common moral judgments (deontology/contractualism), or epistemic humility (skepticism).
Let’s do a deep dive into the redescription problem. How can we prevent someone from just saying “I intend only the good” in any case?

You need constraints on admissible intention descriptions. Several strategies: 1) Plan-based accounts: Intention is defined by the agent’s plan structure—subgoals and means-end reasoning. If the harm is a subgoal or a necessary step, it’s intended. 2) Counterfactual tests: If the agent would not choose the action if the harm did not occur, that suggests the harm is intended (as means). In fat man, if the person didn’t get hit, the trolley wouldn’t stop, so you wouldn’t push. In switch, you would still divert even if the side track were empty. 3) Deliberative indispensability: Harm is intended if the agent must rely on it to justify the action to themselves as an effective route. Again, this is close to counterfactual dependence. 4) Public reason constraints: intentions that cannot be publicly avowed without undermining the justification are suspicious. For example, “I didn’t intend civilian deaths” when you chose a tactic known to kill civilians in order to pressure the enemy may not be credible. 5) Causal pathway constraint: define “intended as means” in terms of causal intermediacy. If the harm is on the causal path to the good, it’s intended as a means (or at least “instrumental”). Each has issues. Counterfactual tests can misclassify cases where harm is inevitable but not used. Causal pathway tests can misclassify cases where something is causally intermediate but not “used” in a morally relevant way. But combining them can reduce redescription arbitrariness.
Could we formalize it with something like Pearl’s causality—interventions and do-calculus?

Yes, in principle. You can model the scenario with structural causal models (SCMs). Let variables: A = action (pull lever / don’t) T = trolley path (main / side) H1 = harm to one (0/1) H5 = harm to five (0/1) S = trolley stops before reaching five (0/1) In standard switch: A -> T T -> H1 and H5 No variable S that depends on H1. In loop: A -> T T -> H1 H1 -> S S -> H5 (prevented) So H1 is an intermediate cause of preventing H5. Then define “using as means” as: the good outcome (not H5) is causally dependent on H1 under the chosen action, meaning that under an intervention do(H1=0) while holding A fixed, the good outcome would not occur. That gives a crisp condition: if preventing harm to five depends on harm to one, then the one is used as means. This seems promising, though you still need to map that technical condition to a moral rule. Also, the model depends on how you represent variables. If you represent “trolley hits obstacle” rather than “person dies,” you might shift intermediacy. But the dependence on the person’s presence remains. So SCMs can clarify the structure and reduce handwaving.
Nice. But then is the switch case really not dependent on H1? If the side track were empty, H1=0 and still H5=0. So yes, not dependent.

Exactly. In the standard switch, do(H1=0) doesn’t change H5 given A=pull, because H5 is prevented by T being side, not by H1. In loop, do(H1=0) would make S=0 and thus H5=1, so dependence holds. That’s the technical backbone of the means/side-effect distinction.
Okay. Now what about “moral responsibility”? In switch you are choosing to kill one. Are you responsible for their death in the same way as in push?

Responsibility can be parsed into: - Causal responsibility: did your action cause the death? - Moral responsibility: are you blameworthy? In both switch and push, you are causally responsible for the one’s death (assuming you act). But blameworthiness differs depending on permissibility. If switch is permissible, then you may be responsible without being blameworthy—like a surgeon causing pain during necessary treatment. Some think push makes you more responsible because the causal chain is shorter and more direct. But from a strict causal standpoint, both are direct enough. So responsibility doesn’t solve the moral question; it presupposes it. Still, it explains why people feel different: pushing feels like “I killed him,” lever feels like “I redirected the trolley.”
That sounds like moral psychology again. Is the trolley problem mainly about psychology rather than ethics?

It’s both. The trolley problem began as a tool in normative ethics (Foot, Thomson) to test principles like DDE. But it also became central in moral psychology because it reveals systematic patterns in judgment. For ethics, the value is conceptual: it isolates variables (means, intention, contact, numbers) and tests whether a principle can account for them. For psychology, the value is empirical: it shows how people actually judge and what factors drive judgments. The risk is conflating the two: taking common intuitions as decisive normative evidence. But many philosophers treat intuitions as data points, not verdicts.

Let’s explore a variant: “two switches.” You can divert the trolley onto a side track with one person, but also flip another switch that moves that person off the track safely. However, you can’t flip both because of timing. Does this change anything?

It introduces opportunity cost and tests whether you have a duty to attempt a “no one dies” option. If you can save everyone by flipping the second switch instead of the first, then you should do that. But you stipulated you can’t flip both; you must choose which switch to flip. If flipping the second switch alone moves the person but doesn’t divert the trolley, then it doesn’t save the five. If flipping the first switch alone saves five but kills one, that’s the standard trade-off. What’s new is: you have two distinct actions, each saving a different group. That makes the choice look more like “choose whom to save” rather than “minimize deaths.” If the second switch saves the one but leaves the five to die, most people would still flip the first. But the presence of the second switch can heighten the sense that you are actively choosing against saving the one. This touches on “doing vs allowing” again: by flipping the first switch, you allow the one to remain in harm’s way, but you also redirect the trolley. It’s a good example of how adding options changes the moral salience: you can be criticized for not saving the one when you could, even though saving the one costs five lives.
That feels like it shouldn’t matter: you can’t save everyone, so extra options shouldn’t change the morality. But they do psychologically.

Right. This is related to “menu dependence” in decision theory and moral psychology: the set of available options affects how we evaluate each option. Normatively, many argue morality should be “choice-set independent” in certain ways: if the best option is available, you should take it regardless of inferior alternatives. But some moral theories allow option sets to matter (e.g., because of intentions revealed by choosing among options). For example, if you choose an option that kills one when there’s an alternative that saves the five and the one, then you’re blameworthy. But if that alternative is impossible, you’re not. So in your two-switch case, if there were a way to save everyone but you can’t do it, it shouldn’t affect permissibility. Yet it can affect perceived character: why did you choose the lever rather than attempt something else? This shows how trolley cases can be sensitive to framing.
Another classic is “the fat villain” variant: the person on the bridge is the one who tied the five to the track. Does that change permissibility?

Yes, because it introduces liability and desert. If the person is responsible for the threat, many people judge them more liable to be harmed to prevent the harm they set in motion. This shifts the moral structure: - The person is no longer an innocent bystander. - The action becomes closer to self-defense or defense of others against an aggressor. Many theories that forbid killing innocents allow harming culpable aggressors when necessary and proportionate. So pushing might become permissible if the person is morally liable. That suggests that the original intuition against pushing is partly about innocence and inviolability, not just physical contact. But liability can be complicated: culpability, proportionality, and whether killing is the only way to stop the threat.
That seems to imply the trolley problem is really a bundle of different issues: intentional killing, using as means, liability, fairness, etc. Is there any “core” lesson?

A plausible core lesson is: moral evaluation is sensitive to the structure of agency, not just outcomes. Trolley problems show that many people (and many theories) treat distinctions like: - intending vs foreseeing, - means vs side effect, - doing vs allowing, - targeting vs redirecting, as morally relevant. Whether those distinctions are ultimately justified is debated, but trolley cases force you to articulate what you think morality tracks: outcomes alone, or also the form of action and the relations among persons. Another lesson is methodological: small changes in description can shift intuitions, so we need careful analysis of what features are doing the work.
Let’s push on the “relations among persons” idea. Some philosophers say morality is about relations, not maximizing impersonal value. How does that help with trolley?

Relational ethics views wrongs as violations of what we owe to each other—claims, demands, respect—rather than failures to promote the best overall state. In trolley, the relational question is: what do you owe the one, and what do you owe the five, given that you can’t satisfy both? Pulling the lever might be seen as choosing a course that best respects each person’s claim insofar as possible: you prevent more rights violations (deaths) without singling someone out as a tool. Pushing might be seen as relating to the victim in a way that denies them standing: you treat them as an instrument for others. That’s a different kind of wrong than merely failing to save. So relational views can explain why the manner matters: it expresses a certain relationship—domination, instrumentalization—between agent and victim. This ties to Kant and contractualism.
But isn’t death itself the biggest violation of what we owe someone? How can “instrumentalization” make it worse?

Two replies: 1) Not additive but categorical: It’s not “death plus extra harm,” but a different kind of wrong. Imagine two ways someone might die: as a foreseeable side effect of an unavoidable rescue, versus being intentionally used as a tool. Both end in death, but the second involves a distinct disrespect. 2) Assurance and social standing: A principle permitting instrumental killing changes how people can relate to one another. Even if you never end up being sacrificed, living under that principle undermines your security as an equal. That’s a relational harm not captured by counting deaths. Still, the pushback remains: can a “different kind of wrong” outweigh multiple deaths? That’s where thresholds and pluralism come in. Some say yes; some say no.
I’m curious about whether the trolley problem is culturally universal. Do people in different cultures show the same lever/push split?

Cross-cultural studies suggest there is a broadly similar pattern—more willingness to divert than to push—but the strength of the asymmetry varies. Factors like religiosity, collectivism/individualism, and exposure to certain norms can shift responses. However, interpreting these differences is delicate: - Different cultures may interpret the scenario differently (e.g., assumptions about authority, fate, or social roles). - Translation and framing matter a lot. - The very act of answering a hypothetical can be culturally inflected. So while the lever/push split is not purely Western, it’s not perfectly universal either.
Let’s consider a logical angle. Is there an inconsistency in saying “pulling is permissible, pushing is impermissible” if both minimize deaths?

Not logically inconsistent, but it requires a non-consequentialist axiom: that some actions are forbidden even when they yield better outcomes. If your only moral principle were “always minimize deaths,” then yes, you’d be inconsistent. But if you add a constraint like “do not intentionally kill as a means,” then the two judgments can be consistent because the actions fall on different sides of the constraint. So the logical structure is: - Principle 1: minimize deaths, ceteris paribus. - Principle 2: do not use persons as mere means (or do not intentionally kill innocents). - Lexical priority or strong weighting of principle 2 over principle 1. Then “pull” is allowed because it satisfies principle 2 (on that interpretation), and “push” is forbidden because it violates it. The philosophical work is justifying principle 2 and its priority.
Could we derive principle 2 from something like a contradiction in universalization? Like if everyone could push, it would undermine something?

You can try a Kantian universalization argument: Maxim M: “When I can save more people by killing one as a means, I will do so.” Universalize: everyone may kill as a means when it saves more. Would that generate a contradiction in conception or will? Some argue it undermines the security necessary for agency: if anyone may kill you as a tool whenever it benefits others, you can’t reliably pursue your ends. That could be a contradiction in will: you couldn’t rationally will a world where your existence is contingent on others’ cost-benefit calculations. But a critic might say: you could will it if the rule is symmetric and reduces overall risk of death; ex ante you might prefer it. That pulls toward contractualist reasoning again. Kantians respond: even if you’d prefer it ex ante, it treats persons as having conditional value, which is inconsistent with valuing humanity as an end in itself. So universalization arguments can support the constraint, but they depend on controversial premises about what rational willing entails.
That brings us back to “humanity as an end.” Can you unpack what “mere means” really means? Because in everyday life we use people as means all the time—hiring a plumber, etc.

Kant’s idea is not “never treat people as means,” because cooperation involves using each other’s services. It’s “never treat people merely as means,” meaning: never involve them in your plans in a way that they could not in principle consent to, or that bypasses their rational agency. Hiring a plumber uses them as a means (they fix your pipe), but not merely as a means, because you engage them through consent and fair terms, respecting them as an agent with their own ends. Pushing someone off a bridge to stop a trolley uses them merely as a means because: - It involves them in your plan without consent. - It treats their body as an object (a brake). - It destroys their agency entirely. The lever case, again, is contested: you still involve someone without consent, but you are not using their body as a tool; you are making a tragic choice under constraint. So “mere means” is about the mode of involvement: coercive, deceptive, violent, bypassing agency.
But in trolley, consent is impossible. So Kant would forbid both lever and push because neither victim consents?

Some strict Kantians might indeed forbid both, arguing you may not intentionally kill innocents without consent. Others argue that consent is not the only criterion. If harm is unavoidable and you are not using someone as a tool, you might permissibly choose the option that preserves more agency overall. There’s also the idea of “hypothetical consent”: what would rational agents agree to under fair conditions? That starts to resemble contractualism. So Kantianism doesn’t yield a single clean answer; it depends on interpretation.
I want to see if there’s a way to defend pushing without being a utilitarian. Is there?

Yes, a few non-utilitarian routes: 1) Threshold deontology: pushing is normally impermissible, but in the 5 vs 1 case, the threshold is met. Some might set the threshold low. 2) Agent-neutral deontology with aggregation: some deontologists allow aggregating rights violations. They might say: violating one person’s right to life is less wrong than violating five persons’ rights to life, even if both are violations. Then you should push. 3) Comparative justice: treat it as minimizing injustice. If someone must die, it is less unjust that one dies than five, regardless of means. 4) “Numbers count” contractualism: if the complaint of death is the same type, then five identical complaints outweigh one, so you should push. These views often still resist transplant by adding role-based constraints or by distinguishing pushing from medical killing. But that can look ad hoc. So you can defend pushing without full utilitarianism, but you often need to relax the strong constraint against instrumental killing.
If you relax it, don’t you slide into endorsing transplant too?

That’s the slippery slope challenge. To avoid it, you need a principled distinction between pushing and transplant. Possible distinctions: - Emergency immediacy: trolley is an imminent threat; transplant is a planned killing. - Institutional roles: doctors have special duties; bystanders don’t. - Consent expectations: people enter hospitals expecting care, not sacrifice. - Causal agency: in transplant you actively create a new lethal threat; in trolley you respond to an existing threat. But each of these can be contested. The more exceptions you add, the more complex your theory becomes. This is why transplant is a stress test: it forces consistency.
This is getting dense, but good. Let’s switch to logic: can we represent these moral principles in deontic logic and see if they’re consistent?

You can attempt a deontic logic representation, though standard deontic logic (SDL) struggles with contrary-to-duty obligations and conflicts. Let: - O(a): it is obligatory to do a. - F(a): it is forbidden to do a. - P(a): it is permissible to do a. You might encode: 1) O(minimize deaths) as a general obligation. 2) F(kill-as-means) as a constraint. 3) In trolley switch, action L (lever) satisfies not kill-as-means, so P(L). 4) In fat man, action Psh violates kill-as-means, so F(Psh). But formalizing “kill-as-means” requires more than propositional logic; you need action descriptions with causal structure or intention operators. Some deontic logics include agency and intention (e.g., STIT logics—“seeing to it that”). Using STIT: - [agent stit: X] means the agent sees to it that X. You might represent: - F([agent stit: death_of_one] when death_of_one is a means to saving_five). But again, you need a way to express “means.” That suggests combining deontic logic with causal logic. Consistency-wise, you can keep it consistent if you allow “moral dilemmas” where obligations conflict, or you prioritize constraints lexically. However, formal systems often reveal hidden assumptions: e.g., “ought implies can,” whether there is always a permissible option, etc.
Do trolley cases create genuine moral dilemmas where every option is wrong?

Some philosophers argue yes: in certain forced-choice tragedies, any option involves wrongdoing, and the best you can do is choose the least wrong. On that view: - Pulling the lever wrongs the one. - Not pulling wrongs the five. So you face a dilemma; you incur “moral residue” no matter what. Others argue morality must be action-guiding and consistent, so there must be a right answer (or at least a permissible answer). They deny genuine dilemmas. Trolley cases are often used to motivate the “dilemma” position because they feel like situations where you can’t avoid wrongdoing. If you accept dilemmas, you can explain why people feel guilt even after doing the “right” thing.
That seems psychologically realistic. But normatively, doesn’t “everything is wrong” collapse guidance?

It can, unless you distinguish: - “Wrong” in the sense of violating a duty, - “All-things-considered right” in the sense of best permissible choice. Some dilemma theorists say you can have conflicting duties, and whichever you choose you violate one. Still, you might have a meta-duty to minimize violation, so you should pull. You’re still blameworthy in a limited sense, but less so. Others say you’re not blameworthy if you choose the best option in a dilemma; the wrongdoing is “tragic” rather than culpable. So guidance can be preserved by adding layers: primary duties can conflict, but secondary principles guide choice.
Let’s step back. If you had to teach this to someone with no background, what’s the cleanest explanation for the lever/push difference?

The cleanest explanation that matches many intuitions is: Pulling the lever redirects a threat so that fewer people die, and the one person’s death is a foreseen side effect, not something you use to save the five. Pushing the person kills them as the method of saving the five; you treat them as an instrument, which violates a strong constraint against using people as mere means. That’s the core. Then you add: some disagree and think only outcomes matter; others think both are wrong; and some think the difference is psychological rather than moral.
Good. Now I want to explore whether the “means” constraint can be justified without appealing to intuition at all. Is there an argument from rational agency?

A constructivist-style argument might go like this: 1) To be an agent is to regard oneself as having reasons and as capable of setting ends. 2) A basic condition of agency is that one’s status as an end-setter is recognized by others; otherwise one is vulnerable to being treated as a mere tool. 3) Principles that permit using persons as mere means undermine the mutual recognition necessary for a community of agents—because they allow overriding someone’s agency whenever beneficial. 4) Therefore, rational agents must endorse constraints against instrumentalizing others, as a condition of living together as agents. This is not purely intuition-free, but it tries to ground the constraint in what it is to be an agent among agents. A critic might say: why does agency require that strong a constraint? Perhaps agency can exist under a utilitarian regime as long as overall welfare is high. The constructivist would reply that agency includes a demand for non-domination and recognition, not just survival. So you can build an argument, but it will rely on substantive claims about the nature of agency and recognition.
That sounds close to Hegelian recognition. Interesting. But then why would the lever case be okay? It still sacrifices someone.

The constructivist would say: the lever case can be framed as an unavoidable conflict where any principle will result in someone’s death; the question is which principle best preserves mutual recognition under tragic constraints. A principle permitting redirection to minimize harm might be acceptable because it doesn’t authorize treating anyone as a tool; it’s a principle for handling unavoidable disasters. A principle permitting instrumental killing (pushing) crosses a line: it treats someone’s body as a resource. So the difference is between: - A tragic allocation of unavoidable harm under a rule that preserves equal standing, - Versus a rule that allows instrumentalization. Again, the challenge is explaining why instrumentalization is categorically worse than being selected to die. The recognition theorist says: because instrumentalization expresses a relation of domination.
I think I’m getting it. One last thing: some argue the trolley problem is misleading because real life rarely has such certainty and binary choices. Does that invalidate its use?

It limits what you can infer, but it doesn’t invalidate it. Thought experiments are like idealized models in science: they strip away noise to isolate variables. The trolley problem isolates features like intentionality and instrumentalization. But idealization can mislead if the stripped-away context is morally essential. For example: - Real decisions involve uncertainty, alternative actions, and institutional roles. - Moral emotions and character matter over time. - Social norms and precedent effects matter. So trolley cases are best used as probes of conceptual commitments, not as direct guides to policy.
That’s a good stopping point. If I want to continue later, I’d like to explore how all this connects to moral realism vs anti-realism.

That’s a natural next step: whether trolley intuitions track objective moral facts, constructed norms, or evolved heuristics. When you return, we can examine how each metaethical view interprets the disagreement between utilitarians, deontologists, and contractualists, and what that implies about the evidential role of trolley cases.
Both Sartre and Camus deal with meaninglessness, but they had a falling out. What's the actual philosophical disagreement?

The disagreement is partly personal and political, but it rests on distinct philosophical temperaments and commitments. 1) The “absurd” and what it implies: - Camus treats the absurd as a permanent structure: the clash between the human demand for meaning/clarity and the world’s indifference. He thinks lucidity requires we not resolve that clash by smuggling in metaphysical consolation (religion, teleology, historical necessity). The right response is “revolt”: a sustained, non-escapist defiance that refuses both resignation and false reconciliation. - Sartre, especially in the 1940s, shares the sense that the world lacks pre-given meaning, but he doesn’t keep “the absurd” as a terminal concept. He shifts quickly to a theory of freedom and project: meaning is made through choice, commitment, and action. Where Camus wants to preserve the tension without synthesis, Sartre is more willing to “construct” meaning through praxis and, later, through historical analysis. 2) Ethics and politics: - Camus is wary of revolutionary politics that justify violence via an end-state (utopia, historical destiny). In The Rebel, he argues that once rebellion becomes “revolution” in the strong sense—claiming a final meaning for history—it tends to rationalize terror. He wants limits: rebellion without totalization. - Sartre, particularly around the early 1950s, is more sympathetic to Marxism as the “horizon” of the age, and he is willing to consider that politics may demand harsh choices under conditions of oppression. He doesn’t celebrate terror as such, but he is more open to the idea that violence may be historically forced, and he is less convinced that “limits” can be maintained cleanly in real struggle. 3) The rupture: Camus’s The Rebel (1951) criticizes historical teleology and revolutionary violence; Sartre’s journal Les Temps Modernes published a harsh review (by Francis Jeanson) framing Camus as politically naïve and philosophically inconsistent. Camus felt misread and morally attacked; Sartre backed the review and the exchange escalated. If you want the “actual philosophical disagreement” in one line: Camus defends a lucid, limited revolt that refuses historical salvation; Sartre tends to treat freedom as realized through committed projects and (at times) through history’s collective struggles, even when that invites compromises Camus thinks corrupt revolt into justification.
When you say Camus refuses “historical salvation,” what exactly is he rejecting? Is it just religion relocated into politics?

Yes—Camus thinks a certain pattern of thought migrates from theology into secular ideologies. By “historical salvation” he means the idea that history has an ultimate meaning or end (a telos) that can justify present suffering and violence as necessary stages on the way to redemption. He’s rejecting several linked claims: 1) Teleology: that history is moving toward a final reconciliation (classless society, perfected humanity, the end of alienation) in a way that gives history a quasi-providential structure. Even if one denies God, the narrative can function like providence: whatever happens is “for” the end. 2) Theodicy-like justification: in theology, theodicy tries to justify evil under a divine plan. In politics, a “secular theodicy” says: atrocities are regrettable but necessary; they will be redeemed by the future. Camus thinks that’s a moral trap because it treats actual persons as expendable for an abstraction (“History”). 3) Epistemic certainty: revolutionary ideologies often claim privileged access to the meaning of history (scientific socialism, dialectical necessity). Camus doubts we can know such necessity; he sees it as metaphysical overreach. So it’s not merely “religion relocated into politics” in a superficial sense; it’s a structural critique: the same logic of justification and consolation reappears. Camus wants rebellion to remain anchored in present suffering and in a demand for justice that does not claim total knowledge of the future.
But doesn’t Camus still make a value claim—like “human life has dignity” or “murder is wrong”? Where does that come from if he rejects metaphysics?

He does make value claims, and this is one of the most debated aspects of his position. Camus tries to ground normativity without appealing to transcendent metaphysics by appealing to a phenomenology of revolt. His basic move is: revolt is not just a feeling; it has an implicit logic. When someone says “no” to oppression—“this is intolerable”—they are also saying “there is a limit” and “there is something in me (and by extension in others) that must not be violated.” That “something” is not a metaphysical essence in the traditional sense; it’s disclosed in the act of revolt itself. Several steps in his reasoning: 1) The “no” implies a “yes”: Revolt denies a condition but affirms a value—some conception of human worth or measure. 2) Universality by implication: If I revolt against being treated as a thing, I’m implicitly appealing to a standard that I can generalize: I’m not merely stating a personal preference; I’m invoking a claim about what ought not be done to humans as such. 3) Limits: Because revolt begins from solidarity in shared vulnerability, it should not turn into unlimited license. That yields his emphasis on measure (la mesure): refuse injustice without becoming the mirror image of the oppressor. Critics (including Sartreans) argue this smuggles in a human nature or moral realism. Camus would respond that he is not deducing values from a metaphysical blueprint; he is articulating what is already presupposed in the lived experience of refusing humiliation and murder. Whether that counts as “metaphysical” depends on how strict you are about what metaphysics includes.
How does Sartre respond to that? Does he think Camus is being inconsistent, or just politically timid?

In the early-1950s polemics, the Sartrean camp tends to push two accusations: inconsistency and political inadequacy. 1) Inconsistency (philosophical): From a Sartrean existentialist angle, values are not “discovered” in revolt as if they were there waiting; they are constituted through choice. Sartre’s slogan is that existence precedes essence: there is no given human nature that grounds universal norms. So when Camus speaks as if revolt reveals a common human measure, Sartreans suspect he’s reintroducing an essence—“human dignity” as a shared substrate—without admitting it. But Sartre’s own view is nuanced: he thinks our choices aim at universality in a certain sense (choosing for oneself is choosing an image of humanity), yet that universality is a feature of commitment, not a pre-given moral law. So he reads Camus as wanting moral universals without doing the existentialist work of showing how they arise from freedom. 2) Political inadequacy (practical-historical): In the postwar context—colonial wars, class conflict, the Cold War—Sartre’s circle argues that refusing revolutionary means on moral grounds can function as complicity with the status quo. They think Camus’s insistence on “limits” can translate into liberal moralism: condemning violence in the abstract while ignoring structural violence. The Jeanson review in Les Temps Modernes essentially frames Camus as someone who wants clean hands in a dirty world, and who critiques Marxism without offering an effective alternative. Camus replies that this is precisely the logic he rejects: sacrificing present persons to an alleged historical necessity. So Sartre’s response is both: Camus is philosophically muddled about where norms come from, and politically insufficiently attentive to the realities that force tragic choices.
I want to be careful here: Sartre isn’t just “ends justify means,” right? What’s his actual ethical framework when it comes to violence?

Right—reducing Sartre to “ends justify means” is too crude. His position shifts over time and is often framed in terms of responsibility under conditions not of one’s choosing. Key elements: 1) Freedom and situation: Sartre insists freedom is always “in situation.” You don’t choose the facts (poverty, occupation, colonial domination), but you choose how to respond. Ethical judgment must attend to constraints and power relations, not just abstract norms. 2) Responsibility without alibis: Even under constraint, you are responsible for what you do. That doesn’t yield a neat rule like “never use violence” or “violence is fine.” It yields a demand: own your choice and its consequences. Sartre thinks bad faith often appears as pretending one has no choice (“history made me do it”) or pretending one is pure (“I refuse any complicity”). 3) Collective struggle and counter-violence: In anti-colonial contexts, Sartre can treat violence as a response to prior violence embedded in oppression. His most notorious rhetoric is in the preface to Fanon’s The Wretched of the Earth (1961), where he depicts decolonial violence as a cathartic reversal of dehumanization. But even there, the philosophical claim is not that violence is intrinsically good; it’s that the colonized are placed in a world where violence structures relations, and liberation may not be achievable by moral appeals alone. 4) Later attempt at synthesis: In Critique of Dialectical Reason (1960), Sartre tries to integrate existential freedom with Marxist analysis. He criticizes deterministic Marxism, but still sees class struggle and material scarcity as shaping possibilities. Ethics becomes entangled with praxis: what actions realistically transform conditions that deny freedom? So his framework is less a simple consequentialism and more a tragic ethics of action: in a violent world, abstention can also be a choice with consequences. Camus wants a principled limit; Sartre worries that “limit” can become a comfort that leaves domination intact.
How does Camus’s “revolt” differ from Sartre’s “commitment” (engagement)? They sound similar—both are about acting.

They overlap in opposing quietism, but they diverge in what action is meant to affirm and how it relates to meaning. Camus: revolt as stance within the absurd - Ontological background: the world is not rationally ordered for us; the absurd is the permanent mismatch between our demand for meaning and the world’s silence. - Action’s role: revolt is a continuous refusal of resignation and false hope. It is not aimed at final reconciliation; it’s a way of living lucidly, affirming life without metaphysical guarantees. - Ethical style: “measure” and limits. Revolt is bounded by the refusal to become murderer in the name of justice. It seeks solidarity and concrete alleviation without claiming to solve history. Sartre: commitment as project that constitutes meaning - Ontological background: there is no pre-given meaning; consciousness is a “nothingness” that projects itself toward ends. Meaning arises through projects. - Action’s role: engagement is not just defiance; it is the creation of values through commitment. You make yourself by what you do, and you shape the world with others. - Ethical style: authenticity requires acting in ways that recognize others as freedoms. But in practice Sartre often treats political engagement as unavoidable: you are responsible for your position in social conflicts. A shorthand: Camus’s revolt is an ongoing “no” that preserves the absurd tension; Sartre’s engagement is a “yes” to a project that seeks to transform the world and thereby constitutes meaning. Camus fears projects become totalizing; Sartre fears revolt without project becomes morally eloquent but politically inert.
In terms of logic, is Camus basically arguing that the absurd makes any “final” justification invalid? Like an anti-foundational argument?

Camus is anti-foundational in the sense that he rejects ultimate justifications that claim to close the question of meaning. But he isn’t a pure skeptic; he wants a disciplined middle position: no ultimate foundation, yet not “anything goes.” Logically, you can reconstruct his stance as a set of constraints rather than a positive foundation: 1) Constraint against transcendence: No appeal to a realm (God, Platonic forms, historical destiny) that would reconcile suffering by giving it a final meaning. This is a prohibition on certain kinds of premises in ethical-political reasoning. 2) Constraint against totalization: No claim to total knowledge of history sufficient to justify present sacrifices. Epistemically, we lack the standpoint required for that inference. 3) Constraint in favor of immanence: Justification must remain tied to lived experience—suffering, injustice, solidarity. That’s not a foundation in the sense of an axiom guaranteeing all conclusions, but it is a methodological anchoring. 4) Minimal normative inference from revolt: From “I revolt” Camus infers “there is a limit” and “there is a common value.” This is the most contestable step. He treats it almost as a transcendental argument: the practice of revolt presupposes some shared measure; otherwise revolt collapses into mere preference or nihilism. So yes: he blocks “final justification” arguments. But he still tries to secure a limited, non-totalizing normativity—more like a set of guardrails than a deductive system.
Sartre’s existentialism is often caricatured as radical subjectivism. But you said he has a kind of universality (“choosing for all”). Can you unpack that without turning it into Kant?

Sartre’s universality is not Kantian in the sense of deriving duties from a formal moral law. It’s closer to a claim about what it means to choose at all. In Existentialism Is a Humanism (1945), Sartre argues: 1) In choosing myself, I choose an image of the human: When I decide what to do, I’m implicitly endorsing that this is a valuable way for a human to be. I can’t coherently treat my choice as meaningful for me while denying it has any normative dimension. 2) The universality is existential, not formal: It’s not: “act only on maxims you can universalize by rational test.” It’s: “your choice sets a standard you implicitly propose.” You are responsible not only for yourself but for the humanity you project. 3) No guarantee of convergence: Unlike Kant, Sartre doesn’t think rational agents will converge on the same maxims. There is no a priori harmony. The universality is a burden—anguish—because you cannot escape the fact that your choices matter as exemplars, but you also cannot justify them by appeal to an objective moral law. 4) Inter-subjective check via the other: Sartre’s account of “the Look” and being-for-others complicates things: others are not just objects affected by my choice; they are freedoms who evaluate and resist. Ethical life becomes a negotiation among freedoms rather than application of a law. So Sartrean universality is a phenomenological-ethical claim about the structure of choosing and being seen by others, not a Kantian derivation of categorical duties.
Historically, what was the timeline? People cite The Stranger and Nausea, but those are novels. When do the philosophical differences crystallize?

A useful timeline separates literary articulation from explicit philosophical/political polemic: 1930s–early 1940s: parallel developments - Sartre: Nausea (1938) dramatizes contingency and nausea at existence. Being and Nothingness (1943) gives the ontology: consciousness as nothingness, freedom, bad faith, being-for-others. - Camus: The Stranger (1942) and The Myth of Sisyphus (1942) articulate the absurd and the refusal of philosophical suicide. Camus’s absurd cycle emphasizes lucidity and revolt as a stance. Mid-1940s: friendship and shared anti-fascist context - Both are associated with the French Resistance milieu and postwar literary-intellectual life. - Sartre’s existentialism becomes a public movement; Camus is allied but not a doctrinaire existentialist. Late 1940s: politics intensify - Cold War polarization, debates about communism, colonialism, and the role of intellectuals. - Sartre moves toward Marxism as an indispensable framework; Camus becomes increasingly critical of Stalinism and revolutionary justification. 1951–1952: crystallization and rupture - Camus publishes The Rebel (1951): a genealogy of rebellion that critiques revolutionary metaphysics and terror. - Les Temps Modernes publishes Jeanson’s review (1952), attacking Camus. - Camus responds with an open letter; Sartre replies; their relationship collapses publicly. 1960s: later divergences continue - Sartre’s Critique of Dialectical Reason (1960) attempts a systematic synthesis. - Camus dies in 1960; his later work (e.g., The Fall, 1956) explores guilt, judgment, and hypocrisy in a different register than Sartre’s political theory. So the explicit philosophical disagreement becomes publicly definite around The Rebel and the Les Temps Modernes exchange, though the deeper temperamental differences were present earlier.
Can we get more technical about “absurd” vs “contingency”? Are these the same concept under different labels?

They’re related but not identical, and the difference matters. Sartre: contingency (and nausea) - Contingency is the fact that beings are “there” without necessity: they have no reason to be as they are. In Nausea, this is experienced as the gratuitousness of existence. - Ontologically, contingency is tied to Sartre’s distinction between being-in-itself (en-soi) and being-for-itself (pour-soi). The in-itself is dense, self-identical, without reason; the for-itself is negation, lack, transcendence. - The affective correlate is nausea: the world’s brute being overwhelms our conceptual categories. Camus: the absurd - The absurd is not just the world’s contingency; it is the relation between human consciousness (which demands meaning, unity, clarity) and the world’s silence. - It’s a “two-term” concept: neither purely subjective nor purely objective, but arising in the encounter. Comparison: - Contingency can be described without reference to human meaning-demand; it’s metaphysical/ontological. Absurd is explicitly relational and existential. - Sartre can treat contingency as a fact from which freedom emerges: because there is no given essence, I must project meaning. Camus treats absurd as a permanent limit: you can live and act, but you cannot honestly claim ultimate meaning. So: overlapping phenomenology (the world as indifferent, ungrounded), different conceptual framing (ontology of being vs relational absurd).
In logic terms, would you say Camus’s argument is more like a reductio against “meaning systems,” whereas Sartre’s is constructive?

That’s a helpful heuristic. Camus often argues by diagnosing “escapes”: - Start with the absurd: we want meaning; the world doesn’t provide it. - Observe strategies that dissolve the tension: religion, metaphysical idealism, certain forms of rationalism, and (in The Rebel) historical teleologies. - Claim these strategies commit a kind of “philosophical suicide”: they deny one of the terms (either the human demand or the world’s silence) to avoid living with the contradiction. This is reductio-like: if you adopt X (a total meaning system), you end up negating the initial lucidity; therefore reject X. Sartre is more constructive: - Begin with the structure of consciousness as negation and project. - Build an account of freedom, bad faith, intersubjectivity, and later praxis and history. - Argue that meaning is constituted through projects; ethics becomes a matter of authentic choice and recognition of others’ freedom. This is more like a positive theory: not merely “don’t do this,” but “here is what we are and how we can act.” That said, Sartre also uses critical arguments (e.g., against determinism, against bad faith rationalizations). And Camus does construct a positive ethos (revolt, measure, solidarity). But the dominant argumentative posture differs: Camus polices the boundaries of lucidity; Sartre elaborates a system of freedom-in-situation.
What about their views on “the Other”? Sartre has the Look; does Camus have anything comparable, or is he more individualistic?

Sartre’s analysis of the Other is more systematic and central to his ontology, but Camus is not merely individualistic; he approaches intersubjectivity through solidarity and moral psychology rather than a formal phenomenology. Sartre: - The Look (le regard) reveals me as an object for another consciousness. It produces shame, pride, alienation—because my being is partly “outside” in the other’s appraisal. - This leads to conflictual structures: I try to recover my freedom by objectifying the other; the other does the same. Relations risk becoming domination (sadism/masochism). - Later Sartre complicates this with reciprocity, groups, and collective praxis. Camus: - In The Myth of Sisyphus, the focus is on individual lucidity and the refusal of suicide; the “absurd man” is often solitary, though not necessarily antisocial. - In The Rebel, solidarity becomes explicit: revolt is said to be “metaphysical” and social—“I rebel, therefore we are.” This is his intersubjective pivot: the experience of revolt reveals a shared condition and generates a community of limits. - In The Plague (1947), solidarity is dramatized through collective response to suffering; the ethical center is decency and persistence rather than heroic self-assertion. So Camus doesn’t have a “Look” equivalent as a technical account of self-other constitution, but he does have a social ontology of sorts grounded in shared vulnerability and the logic of revolt.
I’ve heard people say Camus is closer to the Greeks—measure, limits—while Sartre is modern, Promethean. Is that a fair framing or just a cliché?

It can be a cliché, but it captures something real if handled carefully. Camus and “Greek measure”: - Camus explicitly invokes Mediterranean thought, limits, and a suspicion of hubris. He contrasts “measure” with “excess,” where excess is the drive to total explanation and total transformation. - His admiration for classical balance is also a moral-epistemic posture: accept finitude, avoid absolute claims that license absolutist politics. Sartre and Promethean modernity: - Sartre emphasizes human freedom as world-making. There’s a modernist confidence that meaning is produced, not found. - His political writings often valorize transformative action and historical agency, which can look Promethean: humanity as self-creating through praxis. But the framing can mislead: - Camus is not simply conservative or anti-modern; he supports resistance, condemns oppression, and insists on action. His “measure” is not passivity. - Sartre is not blind to finitude; his analyses of bad faith, alienation, and scarcity acknowledge limits. His “Promethean” rhetoric sometimes outstrips his philosophical caution. So it’s fair as a heuristic about their sensibilities: Camus emphasizes limits and the dangers of totalization; Sartre emphasizes creative freedom and committed transformation.
Can you reconstruct Camus’s critique of Hegel and Marx in The Rebel? What’s the logical chain?

Camus’s critique is a genealogy: he traces how rebellion against injustice can mutate into ideological justification of murder. Hegel and Marx are key nodes because they offer accounts of history as rational/necessary. A simplified chain: 1) Start: metaphysical revolt Camus begins with rebellion against a world that includes suffering and injustice. This can become metaphysical: a protest against creation, God, fate, the given order. 2) The temptation: to replace God with History Once traditional transcendence is rejected, the desire for meaning can reappear as a belief that history itself is the arena of redemption. History becomes the new absolute. 3) Hegel’s contribution (as Camus reads it): - History is rational; contradictions are aufgehoben (sublated) in higher syntheses. - The real is rational: what happens is intelligible as part of the unfolding of Spirit. Camus worries this can be read as: present horrors are moments in a rational process; they will be justified by the end. It grants a retrospective legitimacy to power. 4) Marx’s transformation: - Marx “materializes” the dialectic: class struggle is the motor; communism is the resolution. - Even if Marx begins as a critique of exploitation, the teleological structure can persist: the proletariat becomes the universal class; the revolution is historically necessary. Camus argues that when revolutionaries claim to know the necessary direction of history, they feel authorized to eliminate “enemies of history.” 5) Political consequence: terror as instrument If the end-state is guaranteed (or treated as necessary), then violence becomes a tool justified by historical necessity. This is the secular theodicy: suffering now, salvation later. 6) Camus’s alternative: rebellion without historical absolutism He wants to preserve rebellion’s moral insight—refusal of humiliation—while rejecting the metaphysical-historical guarantee. Hence: limits, measure, and a politics that alleviates suffering without claiming final redemption. Sartreans respond that Camus’s reading of Marx is too monolithic and that conflating Marx with Stalinism ignores material conditions and anti-colonial struggle. Camus replies that the logic of historical justification is the problem, not only its Stalinist implementation.
How does Sartre read Hegel? Does he buy the dialectic? Or is he an existentialist first and only later a Marxist?

Sartre is existentialist first in ontology, but he becomes increasingly interested in dialectical thinking as a way to understand social reality and history. Early Sartre: - Being and Nothingness is not Hegelian in the strong sense, though it engages with Hegel indirectly (especially via Kojève’s influence in France). Sartre’s account of recognition is conflictual and does not culminate in reconciliation. - He is suspicious of totalizing systems that dissolve individual freedom. Postwar to 1960: - Sartre comes to see Marxism as indispensable for understanding institutions, class, and material constraints. But he criticizes deterministic Marxism that treats individuals as mere bearers of structures. - In Critique of Dialectical Reason, he uses dialectical method to analyze how individual praxis becomes collective structures (the “practico-inert”), how scarcity shapes conflict, and how groups form (the “group-in-fusion”). Does he “buy” the Hegelian dialectic? - He adopts dialectical reasoning as a method for grasping dynamic, conflictual totalities, but he resists any dialectic that claims guaranteed reconciliation or an end of history. - His dialectic is grounded in praxis and scarcity rather than Spirit. It’s more open-ended, less metaphysically triumphant. So: existentialist ontology remains primary, but he thinks it must be supplemented by a social-historical analysis that existentialism alone can’t provide.
I’m interested in the term “philosophical suicide.” Is Camus accusing Sartre of that? Or is it aimed at religious thinkers?

In The Myth of Sisyphus, “philosophical suicide” is primarily aimed at thinkers who, after acknowledging the absurd (or at least the limits of reason), then leap to a transcendent solution that dissolves the absurd tension. Camus discusses religious existentialists (e.g., Kierkegaard) as paradigmatic: they see the paradox, then embrace faith as the resolution. Is Sartre accused of philosophical suicide? - Not in the same direct way. Sartre is an atheist; he doesn’t leap to God. - But Camus could be read as thinking Sartre “resolves” the absurd too quickly by turning it into a doctrine of freedom and meaning-making. From Camus’s perspective, declaring that meaning is constituted by projects can look like a secular consolation—an escape from the full weight of absurdity. However, Sartre would deny that: for him, meaning-making is not consolation but burden. Freedom is anguishing; there is no guarantee; projects can fail; others resist. So Camus doesn’t usually label Sartre’s existentialism as philosophical suicide in the strict sense, but the underlying worry is similar: any move that claims to overcome absurdity rather than live within it risks evasion.
Let’s talk about absurdism specifically. People say Camus is “the absurdist.” But did Sartre accept absurdism? Or did he reject the label?

Sartre generally resists being classified as an “absurdist” in the Camus sense, even if his early literature shares the mood. Camus’s absurdism: - Absurd is central and stable: it’s the key to the human condition. The question is how to live with it without appeal to transcendence. - The Myth of Sisyphus explicitly frames the philosophical problem as suicide and answers with revolt. Sartre’s relation: - Sartre’s early fiction can feel absurdist: contingency, nausea, the collapse of rational meaning. - But philosophically, Sartre’s focus is on freedom and intentionality: consciousness is always about something, projecting possibilities. The world’s lack of inherent meaning is the condition for meaning to be made. - Sartre would likely see “absurdism” as too static: it risks portraying humans as trapped in a contradiction rather than as agents who define themselves. So Sartre shares the diagnosis of no pre-given meaning, but he doesn’t make “the absurd” the final frame; he uses it as a springboard toward a theory of action and responsibility.
Can you compare The Myth of Sisyphus to Being and Nothingness in terms of argumentative style? One feels essayistic, the other like a system.

That’s accurate, and it reflects different philosophical ambitions. The Myth of Sisyphus: - Essayistic, literary-philosophical. - Moves through examples (Don Juan, the actor, the conqueror), aphoristic claims, and critiques of other thinkers. - The argument is more like a guided tour of a stance: lucidity, refusal, revolt. - It’s not trying to be a complete ontology; it’s trying to articulate an ethic of living under a condition. Being and Nothingness: - Systematic phenomenological ontology. - Technical distinctions (in-itself/for-itself, facticity/transcendence, bad faith, being-for-others). - Long analyses intended to show how ordinary phenomena (shame, desire, love) reveal ontological structures. - It aims to ground ethics and psychology in a theory of consciousness. This difference matters for the dispute: Sartre’s camp often treats Camus as insufficiently rigorous; Camus treats Sartre’s system-building as prone to abstractions that can morph into ideological justifications.
If Camus is wary of systems, is The Rebel itself a kind of system? It’s a big historical-philosophical sweep.

That’s one of the ironies critics point out. The Rebel is more systematic than Camus’s earlier work: it offers a historical genealogy and a conceptual framework (rebellion → revolution → terror), and it makes broad claims about European intellectual history. Camus would likely distinguish: - A “system” in the pejorative sense: a closed totality that claims to explain everything and legitimates itself by its completeness. - A “framework” or “inquiry” that remains aware of its limits and refuses to totalize. But the line is not always clear, and Sartreans exploit that ambiguity: they argue that Camus condemns totalization while producing grand narratives himself. Camus’s defense is that his narrative is diagnostic and cautionary rather than teleological; it’s meant to show how absolutist logic recurs, not to claim the final meaning of history.
What role did Algeria play in their disagreement? Is it central or more of a later divergence?

Algeria becomes central later, and it highlights their moral-political differences, but it is not the immediate trigger of the 1952 rupture (which is The Rebel). Still, it’s crucial for understanding their divergence in the 1950s. - Camus was born in Algeria (French Algeria) and had a complex position: he opposed colonial injustice and advocated reforms and civil equality, but he also feared both colonial repression and indiscriminate violence. He emphasized protecting civilians and sought a kind of civil truce. - Sartre became a strong supporter of Algerian independence and was willing to defend the FLN’s struggle more unambiguously, interpreting it in anti-colonial and anti-imperialist terms. He condemned French state violence and saw independence as non-negotiable. So Algeria amplifies the earlier pattern: - Camus: limits, refusal of murder, suspicion of revolutionary terror, attention to concrete human costs. - Sartre: anti-colonial liberation as historical necessity; willingness to accept that violent struggle may be unavoidable. Because this topic is politically charged, interpretations often become moralized. Philosophically, it’s another instance of their disagreement over whether ethical limits can be maintained in liberation struggles and whether refusing certain means becomes complicity.
Can you give a more formal schema of Camus’s “limits” argument? Like premises and conclusion, even if it’s not strictly deductive.

One plausible reconstruction (not Camus’s own formalization, but faithful to the thrust) goes like this: P1. The absurd condition precludes any final, transcendent justification of suffering and death (no theodicy, no historical salvation). P2. Revolutionary doctrines that claim historical necessity function as secular justifications of suffering and death (secular theodicy). P3. If an action (e.g., murder/terror) is justified only by an appeal to such a final justification, then that justification is illegitimate (by P1). P4. Therefore, murder/terror cannot be morally justified by appeals to historical necessity or utopian ends. P5. Revolt arises as a refusal of injustice and humiliation, implicitly affirming a shared human value. P6. To preserve that value, revolt must not negate it by treating persons as mere means; otherwise revolt becomes self-contradictory (it denies in others what it affirms in itself). C. Therefore, revolt must be bounded by limits: it must resist injustice while refusing absolute ends that license murder, and it must seek measure rather than total revolution. Points of pressure: - P5–P6: the move from revolt to universal value and non-contradiction can be challenged. - P3: one might argue some violence could be justified by immediate defense rather than final ends. Camus actually allows for tragic necessity in some contexts, but he insists the justification must remain modest, non-totalizing, and haunted by guilt rather than sanctified.
That last part is important: does Camus allow any violence at all? If “murder is wrong,” is he a pacifist?

He’s not a strict pacifist, though he is often read as close to one because of his insistence on limits and his horror at political murder. Camus distinguishes: - Violence as tragic necessity (e.g., self-defense, resistance under occupation) where one may be forced into killing but must not glorify it or turn it into a principle. - Violence as ideological instrument (terror) justified by an absolute end, where killing becomes normalized, bureaucratized, and morally “clean” through doctrine. In The Rebel, he criticizes those who make murder logical—who treat it as a step in an argument. He wants to keep moral experience intact: if one kills, it remains a rupture, a moral wound, not a triumph. So: not pacifism as a rule, but anti-justificationism about murder—especially murder sanctified by historical or metaphysical narratives.
Sartre would say that’s sentimental, right? That in real politics you can’t keep your hands clean?

That’s close to the Sartrean critique as it appeared in the polemics: Camus is accused of wanting purity, of judging from a position insulated from struggle, and of refusing to confront structural violence. But Sartre’s stronger philosophical claim would be: - “Clean hands” is itself a choice that has consequences. If you refuse effective means against oppression, you may be choosing the oppressor by default. - The moral problem is not to remain pure, but to assume responsibility for the compromised choices that situations impose. Camus replies: - The claim that “you must get dirty” easily becomes a blank check. Once you accept that logic, you can always rationalize further violence as necessary. - The real task is to act while resisting the slide into total justification—hence the insistence on limits and on refusing to let ends sanctify means. So the disagreement is not merely about sentiment; it’s about whether moral limits can function as constraints without collapsing into complicity, and whether acknowledging tragic necessity can avoid becoming an ideology.
How do they each think about nihilism? Camus is often called a nihilist by people who haven’t read him.

Both are anti-nihilist, but in different ways. Camus: - He diagnoses nihilism as the collapse of values after the “death of God,” and he thinks some revolutionary ideologies are nihilistic in practice because they destroy present life for an abstract future. - His absurdism is explicitly not nihilism: the absurd does not imply nothing matters; it implies there is no ultimate, pre-given meaning. The response is revolt—affirming life, solidarity, and limits. Sartre: - He rejects nihilism by making freedom constitutive: values exist as what we choose and commit to. There is no objective value “out there,” but that doesn’t mean nothing matters; it means responsibility is inescapable. - Bad faith can look like nihilism: treating values as arbitrary to avoid commitment. Sartre treats that as a flight from freedom. So both oppose “nothing matters.” Camus does it by defending a lived ethos that refuses both suicide and metaphysical consolation; Sartre does it by insisting that meaning is made and that refusing to choose is itself a choice.
Could you connect this to Nietzsche? Both talk about meaning after God. Does Camus align with Nietzsche’s critique of ressentiment? Does Sartre?

Nietzsche is a background presence for both, but they appropriate him differently. Camus and Nietzsche: - Camus admires Nietzsche’s diagnosis of nihilism and the critique of metaphysical consolation. - In The Rebel, Camus engages Nietzsche as a figure of rebellion who tries to affirm life without transcendence. He’s interested in how Nietzsche’s transvaluation can be read as a response to nihilism. - But Camus is cautious about Nietzschean excess: the will-to-power can be read as licensing domination, and the “innocence of becoming” can undercut moral limits. Camus wants affirmation with measure. Sartre and Nietzsche: - Sartre shares Nietzsche’s anti-essentialism and rejection of pre-given values, but he frames it in phenomenology rather than genealogy. - Sartre’s ethics of authenticity has a family resemblance to Nietzschean self-creation, but Sartre emphasizes responsibility toward others as freedoms. Nietzsche is often read as less concerned with universal responsibility. Both are trying to think post-theological meaning, but: - Camus uses Nietzsche partly as a cautionary tale about rebellion turning into metaphysical replacement. - Sartre is closer to the “create values” motif, but he embeds it in intersubjective conflict and responsibility.
What about Kierkegaard? Camus attacks him for the leap of faith, but Sartre is often grouped with Kierkegaard as an existentialist. Do they share anything?

They share the existential focus on lived experience, anxiety, and the individual, but they diverge sharply on transcendence. Kierkegaard: - Emphasizes subjectivity, the leap, faith beyond reason, and the individual’s relation to God. - Anxiety and despair are conditions that can lead to religious faith. Camus: - Respects Kierkegaard’s honesty about the limits of reason but rejects the leap as “philosophical suicide.” For Camus, the leap resolves the absurd by denying reason’s demand for clarity, replacing it with faith. Sartre: - Shares Kierkegaard’s attention to anxiety and individual responsibility, but rejects God and any transcendent grounding. - Sartre’s “anguish” is not a path to faith; it’s the recognition that you are responsible without guarantees. So Sartre and Kierkegaard overlap in existential themes, but Sartre is closer to Camus on atheism, while diverging from Camus on whether atheism leads to a stable “absurd” stance or to a constructive theory of freedom and projects.
If I wanted to be really precise: is Camus an existentialist? He denied it, right?

He denied being an existentialist in the Sartrean sense, and many scholars treat him as adjacent rather than inside the movement. Reasons: - Existentialism (as a public label in Paris) became associated with Sartre’s ontology, freedom, and certain ethical-political commitments. - Camus’s absurdism is distinct: he doesn’t build a phenomenological ontology of consciousness; he focuses on the absurd relation and a moral stance of revolt and measure. - Camus also disliked doctrinal schools and the social scene around “existentialism” as a fashionable movement. Still, there are overlaps: atheism, attention to lived experience, rejection of essentialism, and concern with meaning. If you define existentialism broadly as philosophy centered on existence, freedom, and meaning under finitude, Camus fits. If you define it narrowly as Sartrean/Heideggerian phenomenological ontology, Camus is more of an independent moralist and essayist of the absurd.
Heidegger—where does he fit in? Sartre borrows from him; Camus seems less directly connected.

Heidegger is crucial to Sartre’s philosophical formation and less directly present in Camus. Sartre: - Adopts and transforms Heidegger’s analysis of being-in-the-world, nothingness, and authenticity, but shifts it toward a philosophy of consciousness and freedom that Heidegger himself disliked. - Sartre’s “nothingness” has Heideggerian roots, but Sartre makes it a structure of consciousness (negation), whereas Heidegger treats it as disclosed in moods like anxiety and as tied to Being. Camus: - Reads some phenomenology and existential thought, but his main interlocutors in The Myth of Sisyphus are more eclectic: religious existentialists, rationalists, and literary figures. - His style is less ontological and more ethical-literary; he doesn’t engage Heidegger’s technical vocabulary extensively. So Heidegger is a major background for Sartre’s ontology; Camus’s absurdism can be compared to Heideggerian themes (finitude, meaning, anxiety), but it’s not built as a Heideggerian project.
You mentioned “bad faith.” Does Camus have an equivalent concept? Like self-deception in the face of absurdity?
